known_issues: BT pairing flaky on Windows 11 Home — use USB-BT dongle if onboard radio misbehaves.
notes: STN2100 superset of ELM327. Shop favorite for multi-make work; handles pre-2011 Harley J1850 reliably, unlike most ELM327 clones.

known_issues: Counterfeit ELM327 — firmware usually reports v2.1 but is actually v1.5 silicon. Drops frames on 500 kbit CAN. J1850 support claimed but does not work.
notes: Cheapest option available, ~$13 shipped. Works for basic Mode 03 on modern CAN bikes. Avoid on Harley, avoid for bidirectional actuation. Sold under 50+ brand names (Vgate, Veepeak, BAFX, etc.).

known_issues: WiFi pairing breaks after hotspot changes. Same underlying counterfeit ELM327 silicon as the BT clone. Keep-alive watchdog resets session every ~60s on some firmware.
notes: Same firmware quality problem as the BT variant — just with a different radio. Slightly more convenient for iOS users (BT ELM327s require a paid app like OBD Fusion).

known_issues: FTDI/CH340 chip knockoffs — driver install on Windows 11 requires disabling driver signature enforcement for the cheapest variants.
notes: USB variant of the generic clone. More stable session than BT/WiFi (no wireless drops) but shares the same counterfeit ELM327 firmware problems.

known_issues: STN1110 slightly slower than MX+'s STN2100 on multi-frame CAN reads; noticeable on Mode 22 PID bursts. Otherwise rock-solid.
notes: Mid-tier ScanTool product. Real STN1110 silicon (not an ELM327 clone). Excellent value for shops that don't need the MX+'s peak throughput.

known_issues: USB-only; no wireless option. Linux support via usbserial requires kernel 5.x or newer for reliable USB CDC enumeration.
notes: Entry-level STN1110 — same silicon as LX in USB form. Shop-bench favorite for laptop-tethered diagnostics where wireless is unwanted.

known_issues: ELM327 v1.5 silicon (slightly more selective QC than the $13 clones but still counterfeit). J1850 intermittent; avoid for Harley work.
notes: The least-bad ELM327 clone. BAFX does actual inventory QC so the adapter you receive tends to work; no J1850 support despite the spec sheet claim.

known_issues: BLE stack occasionally needs a power-cycle to reconnect. Mode 22 absent despite some marketing suggesting otherwise.
notes: BLE 4.0 variant — better battery impact than the old BT 2.x Vgate units. Good travel/app-pairing option; not a shop-bench workhorse.

known_issues: Marketed for Ford/Mazda cars — motorcycle support is possible but not the primary use case. HS-CAN + MS-CAN switching requires the FORScan app UI.
notes: STN1110-based with FORScan-optimized firmware. Bidirectional works on cars; motorcycle bidirectional support varies by model.

known_issues: Autel's iOS/Android app is the only way to unlock bidirectional — no raw AT-command passthrough. Motorcycle coverage is limited vs their car database.
notes: Consumer-grade Autel dongle. Good bidirectional car support, partial bike support. Annual software renewal required for full feature set.

known_issues: Standalone scanner — no PC/phone connection. Read-only by design; no bidirectional. Limited motorcycle-specific menus.
notes: Shop-bench handheld. Rugged, works without batteries/phones. Good for quick code pulls on a generic OBD-II bike; not suitable for deep HD-specific diagnostics.

known_issues: Harley-only. Tune slots are consumed on each ECU flash; buying a second-hand PV3 often means the slots are already used (unlock costs ~$100).
notes: Pro-grade Harley tuning + diagnostic tool. Full bidirectional (idle-set, A/F trim, knock test, actuator sweep). Works on pre-2011 J1850 and 2011+ CAN Harleys. Industry standard for Harley tune shops.

known_issues: EFI controller + diagnostic hybrid — replaces the stock Delphi ECM on pre-2011 Harleys. Not a plug-and-play OBD scanner; installation requires full wiring integration.
notes: Aftermarket EFI replacement with built-in datalogger. Pro-level Harley tuning tool. Listed here for completeness — mechanics who run Twin Tec typically pair it with a laptop via their proprietary USB interface, not OBD-II.

known_issues: Harley 2007-present only. Requires VIN unlock per bike via the V&H app; swapping between bikes costs a re-flash.
notes: Harley-specific Bluetooth flasher + diagnostic. Full DTC read/clear + live data + auto-tune pairing with V&H slip-ons. Strong alternative to Dynojet PV3 for shops that run V&H exhaust.

known_issues: Harley-only, smaller vendor (support responsiveness varies). Android app mandatory — no iOS.
notes: Mid-range Harley-specific scanner. Full bidirectional including idle relearn, fuel trim, TPS reset. Popular in independent HD shops for shops that don't need Dynojet's tuning features.

known_issues: Unofficial clones of the Harley-Davidson dealer tool. Firmware quality varies wildly by seller; eBay units often ship with out-of-date firmware. No official update channel.
notes: Clones of the OEM HD dealer tool (DTII, MAT-II). Real dealer access to security-gated functions (key reprogramming, immobilizer sync, ABS bleed). Legal gray area — MoCo actively discourages. Listed for reference; recommend authorized dealer service for these operations.

known_issues: Authorized HD dealers only. Requires annual subscription + Digital Technician II software license. Not available to independent shops via retail channels.
notes: Factory dealer tool — reference only. Full access to all HD diagnostic + security functions. Independent shops cannot legally purchase new units; listed as the 'gold standard' baseline.

known_issues: Simulator / test only — not a real adapter. Phase 144 scenario engine exposes this entry so tests can wire a compat row to the simulated bike fleet.
notes: Reserved slug for the Phase 144 MockAdapter / SimulatedAdapter bridge. Do not purchase — this is the motodiag in-memory test harness, not a shippable product.

known_issues: BLE-only — classic-Bluetooth Windows apps (older OBD II scanners) do not pair with it. iOS works natively; Android works via the OBDLink app.
notes: iOS-focused STN2100 unit. Same silicon as MX+ in BLE form. Mechanics who scan on iPhone/iPad choose this over MX+; classic-BT shops stick with MX+.

known_issues: Mostly marketed for cars/trucks. Motorcycle coverage is generic OBD-II only; make-specific menus are absent. Firmware update process is cumbersome (proprietary PC app).
notes: Budget handheld. Works for basic DTC read/clear on OBD-II-compliant bikes. No PC/phone connection option; truly a standalone device.

known_issues: Launch's motorcycle database is a paid annual add-on. Without it, only generic OBD-II works — no make-specific interpretation.
notes: Shop-bench handheld from Launch (major Chinese diagnostic brand). Good display + navigation; motorcycle add-on database is the real value unlock.

known_issues: BimmerCode bundle is BMW-coding focused — motorcycle features require the BMW Motorrad coding profile (separate in-app purchase).
notes: MX+ hardware paired with the BimmerCode/Motorcycle app. Best consumer option for BMW R1200GS / S1000RR coding work. Hardware is identical to plain MX+ — the value is the software bundle.

known_issues: Authorized Ducati dealers only. Annual subscription + training certificate required.
notes: Factory Ducati dealer tool. Full access to Panigale / Monster / Multistrada diagnostic and service functions. Listed for reference — independent shops need to use an STN2100-based aftermarket option (MX+ with coding app) for comparable depth.

known_issues: Authorized Kawasaki dealers only. Windows laptop with KDS software required; proprietary cable bundle ships with the kit.
notes: Factory Kawasaki dealer diagnostic. Full ZX / Ninja / Versys service menu including ECU update + immobilizer pairing. Reference-only for independent shops.
